question_title: Will any nationwide Spanish vote-intention poll with fieldwork in June 13-27, 2026 show a PP lead over PSOE of 9 points or more?
summary: The question asks if any nationwide Spanish poll with fieldwork ending June 13-27 will show PP leading PSOE by 9+ points. No poll in the current legislature has ever reached this threshold—the maximum was SocioMetrica's +8.0 in May 2026. The private pollster average is ~5.8pp. Monte Carlo simulations modeling SocioMetrica's house effect (~+1.5pp), sampling noise (SE ~1.87pp), 5-6 qualifying polls, and possible Caso Koldo verdict effect consistently give 23-28% for the most conservative parameterization. SocioMetrica alone, with an expected lead of ~7.0-7.5, has roughly a 20% chance of showing 9+ in any single poll, providing the primary path to YES. Other pollsters contribute an additional 5-8% collectively. The Caso Koldo verdict (described as imminent by the Supreme Court on June 5, possibly announced around June 16) could widen the gap further. I estimate 22%, aligning with my conservative simulation outputs.

factors.description: SocioMetrica (most pro-PP pollster with ~+1.5pp house effect) is within striking distance of 9.0 at 8.0, and sampling error (SE ~1.87pp) makes 9+ possible in ~25-30% of any single SocioMetrica poll
Multiple qualifying polls expected (5-6 pollsters with fieldwork ending June 13-27), giving several independent shots at the threshold via maximum-of-N effect
Caso Koldo verdict from Supreme Court described as 'imminent' since June 5, possibly announced around June 16 - could depress PSOE numbers by ~0.5pp in polls fielded during/after announcement
PP-PSOE gap has been gradually widening from ~4pp in early 2025 to ~6pp average in mid-2026, with InvyMark possibly showing PP at 34% (+7 lead) suggesting acceleration
SocioMetrica's May reading of 8.0 may have been noise-elevated (they showed only 5.4 in March, a 2.6pp jump in 2 months) - true expected SocioMetrica reading probably 7.0-7.5, not 8.0
PolitPro trend average shows only 4.1pp gap; private pollster average (excl CIS) is ~5.8pp - the typical poll is 3+ points below the 9.0 threshold, making it a genuine tail event for most pollsters

reasoning:
**Final Forecast: 0.22 (22%)**

**Key reasoning:**

- **Threshold never crossed:** The 9.0pp PP-PSOE lead has never been observed in any poll during the current legislature. The maximum was SocioMetrica's +8.0 in May 2026.

- **Primary path to YES:** SocioMetrica (strongest pro-PP house effect at ~+1.5pp above average) is the most likely pollster to cross 9.0. With sampling error of ~1.87pp, a single SocioMetrica poll has roughly a 25% chance of showing 9+ if their expected lead is ~7.5.

- **Multiple polls help:** With 5-6 qualifying polls expected in the June 13-27 window, the maximum-of-N effect provides multiple independent chances at the threshold.

- **Caso Koldo catalyst:** The Supreme Court verdict on this PSOE corruption case was described as "imminent" since June 5 and may have been announced around June 16, potentially depressing PSOE numbers in polls fielded during/after the announcement.

- **Conservative quantitative basis:** Monte Carlo simulations across various parameterizations consistently produced 23-28% for conservative assumptions. My 22% estimate sits at the conservative floor of these computational results.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will any nationwide Spanish vote-intention poll with fieldwork in June 13-27, 2026 show a PP lead over PSOE of 9 points or more?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44103)
[Opinion polling for the next Spanish general election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_next_Spanish_general_election)
[Leadership opinion polling for the next Spanish general election - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leadership_opinion_polling_for_the_next_Spanish_general_election)
[Election Polls in Spain: All Recent Surveys at a Glance](https://politpro.eu/en/spain/opinion-polls)
[Latest polls and surveys of general elections in Spain](https://www.democrata.es/en/center-of-surveys-and-electoral-polls/general-elections/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if at least one nationwide (Spain-wide, Congreso de los Diputados) vote-intention poll, with fieldwork ending between June 13 and June 27, 2026 (inclusive), is published and listed on Wikipedia's "Opinion polling for the next Spanish general election" page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_next_Spanish_general_election) in the nationwide polling table, in which the estimated vote-intention percentage for the Partido Popular (PP) exceeds that of the Partido Socialista Obrero Espanol (PSOE) by 9.0 percentage points or more. Otherwise it resolves NO. The "lead" is computed as the PP figure minus the PSOE figure as shown in that table's columns. If a poll lists both a "raw" and an "adjusted/estimate" figure, the figure shown in the main nationwide voting-intention table (the headline estimate) is used.
fine_print: "Fieldwork ending between June 13 and June 27, 2026" refers to the last date of the fieldwork period shown in the Wikipedia table's "Fieldwork date" column for that poll's row. A poll qualifies only if that end date falls on or within June 13-27, 2026. Only nationwide polls for the Congress of Deputies count; regional, European, municipal, or leadership/approval polls do not. If multiple qualifying polls exist, the question resolves YES if any one of them shows a PP-minus-PSOE lead of 9.0 points or greater. The CIS barometer counts as a poll if it appears in the table with a qualifying fieldwork end date. If the Wikipedia page is unavailable at resolution, the resolver may use the underlying poll as reported by the polling firm or by credible sources (https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). If no qualifying poll has been published by June 28, 2026, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Maximum PP-PSOE lead ever observed in current legislature is 8.0 (SocioMetrica, May 2026), still below the 9.0 threshold
SocioMetrica (most pro-PP pollster with ~+1.5pp house effect) is within striking distance of 9.0, and sampling error (SE ~1.87pp) makes 9+ possible in any given poll
Multiple qualifying polls expected (5-6 pollsters with fieldwork ending June 13-27), giving several independent shots at the threshold
Caso Koldo verdict from Supreme Court described as 'imminent' on June 5, still pending as of June 16-17 - could depress PSOE numbers if it lands during fieldwork window
PP-PSOE gap has been gradually widening (from ~4pp in early 2025 to ~6pp average in mid-2026), but rate of widening may be slowing
SocioMetrica's May reading of 8.0 may itself have been noise-elevated (they showed only 5.4 in March) - true expected SocioMetrica reading is probably 7.0-7.5
PolitPro trend average (including CIS) shows only 4.1pp gap; excluding CIS, private pollster average is ~5.8pp - still 3+ points below threshold

input.anchor: Base rate: No poll in the current legislature (since July 2023) has ever shown PP leading PSOE by 9+ points. This unprecedented threshold suggests a low base rate of approximately 15%.
input.assessment:
The question asks whether any single nationwide Spanish poll with fieldwork ending June 13-27 will show a PP-PSOE gap of 9.0+ points. The maximum observed in the current cycle is SocioMetrica's 8.0 (May 2026), meaning the threshold has never been crossed.

The primary path to YES is through SocioMetrica (El Español), which has the strongest pro-PP house effect (~+1.5pp above average). If they publish a qualifying poll with expected lead ~7.0-7.5, the probability of a single poll crossing 9.0 is about 21-30% (given SE ~1.87pp). With ~75% chance they have a qualifying poll, SocioMetrica alone contributes ~15-22% to the YES probability.

Other pollsters (GAD3, Sigma Dos, Invymark, 40dB, etc.) have expected leads of 4.7-6.5, making it much harder for them to cross 9.0, though with SE ~1.9pp, tail events are possible. Collectively they add ~5-8% to the probability.

The Caso Koldo verdict could widen the gap, but the scandal has been in the news for over a year, so incremental effect is modest (~0.5pp if verdict comes during the window).

Monte Carlo simulations across various parameterizations give 23-33% for this scenario, with the range driven by assumptions about SocioMetrica's true expected lead and the verdict effect. I estimate ~18% after discounting for the fact that the 9.0 threshold is unprecedented and SocioMetrica's 8.0 was likely noise-elevated.
input.calibration_notes: The 9.0 threshold has never been reached in any poll in this legislature. This is strong evidence but not dispositive since the gap has been widening. My estimate of 18% sits below the simulation median (~27%) because I apply a discount for model risk and unprecedented threshold. The hedging check: I'm not at 50%, and my evidence clearly supports below-average probability. Status quo (no poll reaching 9+) resolves NO.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. SocioMetrica's true expected lead: is it 7.0 or 8.0? 2. Whether SocioMetrica will have fieldwork ending in the June 13-27 window. 3. Whether the Caso Koldo verdict will come during the window and its incremental effect. 4. How many total qualifying polls will exist.
input.tool_audit: Useful: web_search (found Sigma Dos June 14 poll, Electomania data, CIS results), fetch_url (got Electomania poll details, Wikipedia page structure), research() (got detailed pollster comparison data). Partially useful: Metaculus question fetch (confirmed no CP available). Empty: some fetch_url attempts on Facebook (HTTP 400). Issues: Wikipedia table data couldn't be fully extracted (tables don't render well in text extraction).
input.update_triggers: 1. If the Caso Koldo guilty verdict is announced in the next few days → +5pp. 2. If I find evidence of a poll already showing 8.5+ with fieldwork in the window → significant upward revision. 3. If SocioMetrica publishes a new poll in the window showing <7 → significant downward revision.
input.process_reflection: The polling threshold question type works well with Monte Carlo simulation. The main friction was extracting actual poll data from Wikipedia's HTML tables - the fetch tools return the text preamble but not the tabular data. The research() sub-agent also couldn't access live web data, limiting its usefulness for time-sensitive polling questions. Direct web_search + fetch_url was more effective for finding individual poll results. The Electomania aggregator site was the most useful source for identifying recent polls.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Resolution Criteria
**Recovered**: Yes — the agent fetched the Metaculus question page and obtained full resolution criteria. The factors and assessment engage correctly with the specific terms (Wikipedia table, fieldwork end dates June 13-27, PP minus PSOE ≥ 9.0, nationwide Congreso polls only). No resolution misalignment.

### Factor-by-Factor Verification

**Factor 1** (max PP-PSOE lead = 8.0, SocioMetrica May 2026): ✅ Verified. The research() result confirms SocioMetrica PP 33.9% vs PSOE 25.9% = +8.0, fieldwork 28-30 May 2026. The Electomania page also confirms this figure.

**Factor 2** (SocioMetrica house effect ~+1.5pp, SE ~1.87pp): ✅ Verified. The simulation code computes SE from sample proportions (pp=33.9, psoe=25.9, n=1200) and arrives at 1.87pp. The house effect of ~1.5-2.0 is reasonably inferred from comparing SocioMetrica (+8.0) vs the private pollster average (~5.8) = ~+2.2, though some of that is real signal.

**Factor 3** (5-6 qualifying pollsters): ✅ Reasonably grounded. The trace identifies 8+ active national pollsters. The estimate of 5-6 with qualifying fieldwork dates is a reasonable inference from monthly polling cadences and a 15-day window.

**Factor 4** (Caso Koldo verdict "imminent," described as "still pending as of June 16-17"): ⚠️ Partially verified with a notable gap. The trace found the moncloa.com June 5 article saying the verdict would be "inmediata." However, the worldview archive entry (accessed June 15) — which the agent's *research()* tool DID NOT retrieve — states the Supreme Court was deliberating on June 15 and the fallo was expected "as early as June 16, 2026." By the time of forecasting (June 17-18), the verdict may have already been announced. The agent treated the verdict as a ~40-50% probability future event during the fieldwork window, which is likely an underestimate of its certainty. This doesn't change the forecast direction (verdict would push toward YES), but the agent may be underweighting this catalyst.

**Factor 5** (gap widening from ~4pp to ~6pp): ✅ Verified. Early 2025 baseline ~3.5pp, mid-2026 private average ~5.8pp.

**Factor 6** (SocioMetrica's 8.0 may be noise-elevated): ✅ Reasonable inference. March was 5.4, May was 8.0 — a 2.6pp jump in two months is large and likely includes some positive noise.

**Factor 7** (PolitPro trend 4.1pp): ✅ Verified. PolitPro fetch shows PP 32%, PSOE 27.9% = 4.1pp. The agent correctly notes this includes CIS which drags the average down.

### No Hallucinated Evidence
All core claims are grounded in the research trace. No factor's main conclusion lacks trace support.

### No Double-Counting
Factors 1 and 6 both reference SocioMetrica's 8.0 reading, but they serve different analytical purposes: Factor 1 establishes the historical ceiling, Factor 6 discounts it as noise. These are complementary rather than double-counted. Factors 2 and 3 are conceptually related (individual poll probability × number of polls), but they reflect genuinely different dimensions.

### No Wrong-Direction Factors
All logit signs align with their descriptions.

### Probability Assessment — Concern (Primary Issue)
The agent's simulations consistently gave **23-42%** across different parameterizations:
- Conservative (no drift, no verdict): 30.7%
- Refined with SocioMetrica expected = 7.0: 23.2%
- Refined with SocioMetrica expected = 7.0 + verdict: 27.7%
- Refined with SocioMetrica expected = 7.5: 27.5%
- Base case: 48.4% (with drift/verdict)
- Factor-implied probability: ~28.3%

The final estimate of **18%** is below the floor of every parameterization the agent ran, including the most conservative one (23.2%). The agent's stated justification — discounting for "unprecedented threshold" and "SocioMetrica's 8.0 was noise-elevated" — reflects **double-discounting**: the conservative simulation (SocioMetrica expected at 7.0) already incorporated both of these adjustments. Setting SocioMetrica's expected lead at 7.0 already accounts for noise elevation, and the 15% base rate anchor already captures the unprecedented nature of the threshold.

The agent's own reflection flagged a gap between factor-implied probability (28.3%) and tentative estimate (18%), and the thinking text shows the agent considered revising to 20% but didn't. The thinking explicitly states "my simulations consistently show 25-35% across different reasonable parameter assumptions, and I should trust the computation rather than manually adjusting downward."

My independent estimate based on the trace evidence: **22-25%**. The most conservative realistic parameterization (SocioMetrica expected ~7.0, other pollsters at observed levels, ~40% verdict probability) gives 23-28%. Even without the verdict, it's 23%. The 18% estimate is ~5pp below the agent's own most conservative model output. While this is not a dramatic divergence, it represents the agent overriding its own quantitative analysis with qualitative hand-waving about "unprecedented thresholds" that the model already captured.

Additionally, the Caso Koldo verdict likely was announced by June 16 (worldview archive says fallo expected June 16), and if polls in the field during/after the verdict announcement show widened gaps, the probability should be somewhat higher than modeled. The agent didn't discover this timing update.

### Weak Counterargument
The agent's counterargument is actually quite strong — it constructs a coherent alternative story and identifies specific evidence (SocioMetrica's proximity, verdict timing, InvyMark movement, multiple-poll effect, simulation consistency). This is not a token gesture. ✅

### Overconfident Self-Assessment
Confidence reported at 0.40, which is appropriate given the uncertainty. ✅

### Tool Audit
The agent states fetch_url had "some empty attempts on Facebook (HTTP 400)." The metrics show fetch_url had 1 error out of 8 calls (12.5%). This is consistent with the agent's description. No disagreement.

### Verdict: WARN
The primary issue is the probability estimate of 18% sitting below the floor of every simulation the agent ran (minimum 23.2%), representing a double-discount of factors already incorporated in the models. This doesn't change the forecast direction (the agent correctly identifies this as a minority-probability event), but the ~5pp gap between the estimate and the agent's own quantitative models represents a meaningful underestimate. A more appropriate estimate would be in the 22-25% range.
